elements.sent-2411-001: Ce-induced effects on the self-assembly of arachidic acid Langmuir monolayers was studied in this work.
elements.sent-2411-002: The monolayers were formed on the liquid subphase in the presence of Ce(III) ions.
elements.sent-2411-003: A new type of structural configuration is found for such monolayers, in which the monolayer maintains its structural ordering despite being compressed beyond the collapse point.
elements.sent-2411-004: Instead of forming 3D aggregates as in the typical collapsed state, the monolayer appears to be corrugated.
elements.sent-2411-005: Grazing incidence X-ray diffraction and X-ray standing waves confirm these findings.
elements.sent-2411-006: The diffraction pattern for the monolayer in a new state is represented by the unclosed diffraction rings with maxima near the sample horizon.
elements.sent-2411-007: This diffraction pattern is quantitatively reproduced in the numerical simulations by assuming the corrugated monolayer.
elements.sent-2411-008: The details of the conditions under which these corrugated Langmuir monolayers were observed and the analysis of the diffraction data are described.
elements.sent-2411-009: Scientific interest in the self-assembly of amphiphilic trivalent lanthanide complexes is motivated by their photophysical properties, which can be exploited for molecular sensing~\cite{dossantos2008Recent .}
elements.sent-2411-010: The practical realization of such systems requires the assembly of the lanthanide complexes into nanostructures~\cite{wales2016surface .}
elements.sent-2411-011: One of the possible ways to create functional nanomaterials with predefined molecular architecture is the Langmuir-Blodgett technique~\cite{ariga2013lb , which involves the self-assembly of amphiphiles at the air-liquid interface into a Langmuir monolayer and subsequent transfer to a solid substrate.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Charged headgroups of amphiphilic molecules are known to interact with metal ions when present in the liquid subphase~\cite{bosio1987reflectivity .}
elements.sent-2411-013: In this way, self-assembly defines the performance of a nanodevice.
elements.sent-2411-014: Self-assembly is driven not only by the sort of amphiphiles and the thermodynamic conditions under which the monolayer is formed~\cite{kaganer1999structure , but can also be directed by interactions with ions in the subphase~\cite{barry2019chiral .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Therefore, further development of such functional devices requires extensive and detailed studies of the structural organization of amphiphilic trivalent lanthanide complexes.
elements.sent-2411-016: There are two known scenarios of how the interaction with metal ions affects the structure of the Langmuir monolayer, as reported in~\cite{kmetko2001effects using divalent metal ions as an example.}
elements.sent-2411-017: The first, more common scenario is that the interaction between headgroups and metal ions leads to the formation of a disordered thin monolayer of metal ions underneath the organic monolayer, which affects the compressibility of the monolayer, resulting in structures similar to high-pressure solid-phase monolayers~\cite{kmetko2001effects .}
elements.sent-2411-018: The second scenario is the formation of the ionic monolayer ordered into a superstructure commensurate with the organic monolayer, which was first reported in~\cite{leveiller1991crystallinity .}
elements.sent-2411-019: The study reports the formation of a Cd$^{2+}$ superstructure underneath an arachidic acid monolayer.
elements.sent-2411-020: The structure has a $2 \times 3$ cell commensurate with the organic monolayer structure.
elements.sent-2411-021: The transition between these two scenarios of interaction of the organic monolayer with metal ions may depend on the pH of the subphase and the ion concentration.
elements.sent-2411-022: In~\cite{dupres2003superlattice , the interaction of behenic acid with a variety of divalent ions was studied, and an ion concentration threshold was observed.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Above this threshold, the ions formed a superlattice, and the ion layer was disordered below it.
elements.sent-2411-024: Later studies~\cite{miller2016observation, miller2017atomic report on forming ordered monolayers of lanthanide ions.}
elements.sent-2411-025: They tested a wide range of trivalent lanthanides.
elements.sent-2411-026: They found that the ordering depends not only on the molecules forming the Langmuir monolayer, but also on the atomic number of a lanthanide.
elements.sent-2411-027: In addition, the crystalline structure of the ionic monolayer can be commensurate or incommensurate with the lattice of the organic molecules.
elements.sent-2411-028: Another interesting aspect is the collapse of Langmuir monolayers in the presence of metal ions.
elements.sent-2411-029: The study in~\cite{zhang2020spontaneous reports the spontaneous collapse of fatty acid monolayers in the presence of Ca$^{2+}$ ions.}
elements.sent-2411-030: It has also been shown both experimentally~\cite{vaknin2007ordering and theoretically~\cite{lorenz2006atomistic that the presence of Ca$^{2+}$ ions changes the structure of the collapsed fatty acid monolayer.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Even more interesting is that the collapsed structures show a high degree of order, namely the formation of inverted bi-layers.
elements.sent-2411-032: The formation of such inverted bi-layers is also reported for lanthanides~\cite{nayak2022spontaneous .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Yet again, the structure of the collapsed layers is ion-specific.
elements.sent-2411-034: Thus, the interaction of fatty acids with lanthanide ions has shown complex and widely varying results.
elements.sent-2411-035: However, understanding the mechanisms of ion monolayer formation and its effect on collapse is relevant to the problems of functionalizing lanthanides into self-assembled nanostructures.
elements.sent-2411-036: This article focuses on the self-assembly of arachidic acid monolayers in the presence of Ce$^{3+}$ ions and their structural reorganization while compressed beyond the collapse point.
elements.sent-2411-037: We have studied the structure of fatty acid monolayers interacting with metal ions at the air-liquid interface at different surface pressures and temperatures.
elements.sent-2411-038: As the primary tool for monitoring the monolayer structure, we used grazing incidence X-ray diffraction (GID) at the bending magnet synchrotron radiation source.
elements.sent-2411-039: This choice is due to the sensitivity of GID to both translational and orientational lateral ordering of the monolayers.
elements.sent-2411-040: In addition, simultaneously with GID experiments, we performed the X-ray standing wave (XSW) measurements to monitor the vertical localization of Ce$^{3+}$ ions during the structural reorganization at the collapse.
elements.sent-2411-041: The isotherm measured on pure water corresponds to a compression with two phase transitions: condensation and solidification.
elements.sent-2411-042: The isotherm measured for a monolayer on a subphase containing Ce$^{3+}$ ions is characteristic of a phase transition from gas to solid with a compressibility of 2.2~m/mN, bypassing the liquid phase.
elements.sent-2411-043: We have studied the behavior of the arachidic acid monolayer, formed on Ce$^{3+}$ solution and on pure water as a control.
elements.sent-2411-044: All measurements were performed at a temperature of $T = 21^\circ$C.
elements.sent-2411-045: The typical $\pi$-$A$ isotherms are shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:iso .}
elements.sent-2411-046: Based on these experimental results, the key feature of the arachidic acid monolayer formed in the presence of Ce$^{3+}$ is the absence of a liquid phase, as evidenced by a high monolayer compressibility -- 2.2~m/mN.
elements.sent-2411-047: Our observations are consistent with the results reported in~\cite{sthoer2022la , where detailed studies of the binding interaction of lanthanide ions (La$^{3+}$ and Y$^{3+}$) with arachidic acid monolayers at submicromolar salt concentrations were reported.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Note that similar changes in the isotherms were observed in~\cite{capistran2019effects , where the effect of Cu$^{2+}$ on the organization and morphology of the arachidic acid monolayer was studied.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Generally, metal binding with carboxylate acid groups is recognized to promote the formation of a rigid monolayer with higher crystallinity and reduced molecular area~\cite{johann2001effect .}
elements.sent-2411-050: The interaction of divalent and trivalent metallic ions with carboxylic groups of fatty acids is strongly dependent on such parameters as solution pH, temperature, salt concentration.
elements.sent-2411-051: The type of metal-carboxylate complex (ionic, unidentate, chelating bridging bidentate, polymeric tridentate) is determined to a large extent by metallic ion specific nature, i.e. ion size, coordination structure, oxidation state in solution, etc. Large Ln$^{3+}$ ions with high coordination numbers exhibit rather intricate binding behavior~\cite{sthoer2022la , resulting in a wide variety of the lanthanide-carboxylate binding interactions.}
elements.sent-2411-052: According to the data reported in~\cite{sthoer2022la , at least three different coordination modes for carboxylate headgroups with lanthanide cations (La$^{3+}$ and Y$^{3+}$) have been identified at the air/water interface.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Furthermore, the relative ratio between the different complexes was found to depend on the salt concentration in the subphase.
elements.sent-2411-054: Thus, the intricacies of the lanthanide complexation behavior impose structural complications on the self-assembly processes in the fatty acid monolayers.
elements.sent-2411-055: The underlying mechanisms regulating these processes are the main focus of our studies.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics{FIGURES/BAM.pdf} \caption{Brewster angle microscopy images of arachidic acid monolayers formed on the aqueous solution of the Ce(NO$_3$)$_3 \, \cdot \,$6H$_2$O salt. The images were taken at different stages of compression: (a) $\pi = 0.4$~mN/m; (b) $\pi = 35$~mN/m; (c) $\pi= 57$~mN/m; (d) $\pi = 58$~mN/m. The growth of the large crystalline-like aggregates can be seen in images (c) and (d) taken beyond the collapse point. The images (a)--(d) were taken at the temperature $T=23^\circ$C. The appearance of the mosaic-like collapse structure at the lower temperature $T=21^\circ$C is shown in (e) and (f). The image (e) was taken just beyond the collapse point, the image (f) was taken when the monolayer was further compressed. } \label{fig:bam}
elements.sent-2411-056: We performed BAM measurements of the arachidic acid monolayer formed on Ce$^{3+}$ solution to visualize the changes in morphological properties at different monolayer phases.
elements.sent-2411-057: The representative BAM images are shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:bam .}
elements.sent-2411-058: As can be seen, the BAM data obtained for the gas and solid phases indicate the formation of a uniform sheet-like layer.
elements.sent-2411-059: This kind of BAM images are usually recorded for fatty acid formed on a subphase with metal ions~\cite{capistran2019effects .}
elements.sent-2411-060: Effects in the phase behavior of the arachidic acid monolayer induced by Ce were observed in BAM measurements in the collapse region (see Fig.~\ref{fig:bam ).}
elements.sent-2411-061: We performed BAM studies of the arachidic acid monolayer at two different temperatures, $T = 21^\circ$C and $T = 23^\circ$C.
elements.sent-2411-062: BAM images taken at $T = 23^\circ$C showed the formation of large 3D crystalline-like aggregates.
elements.sent-2411-063: Such disordered structures are often observed in BAM studies of the collapse behavior of fatty acid monolayers.
elements.sent-2411-064: In contrast, the post-collapse BAM data obtained for the low temperature monolayer ($T=21^\circ$C) revealed the mosaic-like texture of the monolayer.
elements.sent-2411-065: As shown in Figs.~\ref{fig:bam (e),~\ref{fig:bam (f), the monolayer surface is divided into many two-dimensional patches, which can be identified as anisotropic domains that appear homogeneously across the monolayer upon compression.}}
elements.sent-2411-066: It should be emphasized that no growth of these domains into crystalline-like aggregates was observed upon further compression of the monolayer.
elements.sent-2411-067: In addition, BAM images taken at $T=21^\circ$C near the collapse pressure revealed another interesting feature of the deformation processes in the collapse.
elements.sent-2411-068: As can be seen in Fig.~\ref{fig:bam (e), the surface of the monolayer is weakly grooved in the direction parallel to the barrier, which is a clear indication of the preferred deformation direction at the beginning of the collapse.}
elements.sent-2411-069: Taken together, the presented BAM studies of arachidic acid monolayers formed on a subphase containing Ce$^{3+}$, revealed differences in the morphological properties of two observed collapsed modes.
elements.sent-2411-070: Thus, in our further discussion, the collapse with disordered morphology will be referred to as {\it collapse mode~1 ; whereas the collapse with specific mosaic-like morphology will be referred to as {\it collapse mode~2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-071: Note that similar results are reported in~\cite{hatta2003topological , where two qualitatively different collapse morphologies are also observed in fatty acid monolayers.}
elements.sent-2411-072: They used phase contrast microscopy to study the collapse processes in the stearic acid monolayer as a function of the pH of the water subphase.
elements.sent-2411-073: According to the experimental data~\cite{hatta2003topological , the occurrence of collapse structures seen as bright random cracks was detected at $\text{pH =7.8$, while a surface roughening collapse pattern was observed at $\text{pH =7.5$.}}}

elements.subsubsection-2411-001: \bf Control series: monolayer on pure water
elements.sent-2411-101: An arachidic acid monolayer was formed on the surface of pure water and compressed to a surface pressure $\pi = 20$~mN/m at temperature $T = 21^\circ {\rm C}$.
elements.sent-2411-102: In this series of measurements [see Fig.~\ref{fig:all (a)] a typical GID pattern for a Langmuir monolayer was observed: a triplet of peaks $(02)$, $(11)$, and $(1\overline1)$.}
elements.sent-2411-103: The brighter peak at the horizon of the monolayer is the $(02)$ peak, the peak above the horizon is a degenerate peak, in which two peaks $(11)$ and $(1\overline 1)$ are superimposed.
elements.sent-2411-104: The arrangement of the peaks on top of each other indicates a hexagonal lattice: $b = \sqrt{3 a$, $\gamma = 90^\circ$.}
elements.sent-2411-105: The tails of the fatty molecules are inclined with respect to the surface and are tilted in the direction of the nearest neighbor which corresponds to the NN tilt phase mode.
elements.sent-2411-106: We infer this from the arrangement of peaks $(02)$ on the horizon and $(11)$ directly above, which is characteristic of the NN tilt~\cite{kaganer1999structure .}
elements.sent-2411-107: The monolayer has been compressed to a surface pressure $\pi = 20$~mN/m at $T = 23^\circ{\rm C}$.
elements.sent-2411-108: GID data were collected continuously over a 13~hour period in multiple exposures.
elements.sent-2411-109: The integrated GID pattern is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:all (b).}
elements.sent-2411-110: The surface pressure was kept constant at $\pi = 20$~mN/m throughout all exposures.
elements.sent-2411-111: The GID patterns in all exposures are equivalent down to the measurement noise.
elements.sent-2411-112: Thus, the structure is unchanged throughout the 13~hour period.
elements.sent-2411-113: This is quite remarkable because it indicates that the monolayer was not damaged by the radiation.
elements.sent-2411-114: Note that the radiation damage is a typical problem in the synchrotron studies of Langmuir monolayers, see e.g.~\cite{strzalka2004resonant .}
elements.sent-2411-115: Here, we would like to highlight the importance of measuring the GID at lower intensities and with longer exposures, as the self-assembly process may be slower than the degradation induced by radiation.
elements.sent-2411-116: From this point of view, measuring on a bending magnet beamline can be advantageous compared to brighter sources such as insertion devices.
elements.sent-2411-117: In addition to a conventional GID pattern for a fatty acid monolayers in range~C, we observed additional peaks in range~A in this series.
elements.sent-2411-118: In range C we again see a $(02)$ peak at the horizon and a degenerated peak $(11)$, $(1\overline1)$ above the horizon.
elements.sent-2411-119: However, the GID pattern observed in the control series, the $(02)$ peak is shifted horizontally to higher frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-120: This implies a lattice with tighter packing, compared to that of the arachidic monolayer on pure water: the surface area occupied by a molecule is reduced; 20~\AA$^2$ for series~1 versus 20.4~\AA$^2$ for control.
elements.sent-2411-121: The lattice is not hexagonal ($b \neq \sqrt{3 a$), however the lattice angle is the same: $\gamma = 90^\circ$.}
elements.sent-2411-123: Of particular interest are the bright peaks in the range~A.
elements.sent-2411-124: We attribute these peaks to diffraction at a layer formed by Ce$^{3+}$ ions adsorbed at the bottom of the monolayer on the surface of the subphase.
elements.sent-2411-125: This assumption is confirmed by two observations.
elements.sent-2411-126: First, the low spatial frequencies $Q_\parallel$ of the peaks imply a larger spatial period compared to the organic lattice.
elements.sent-2411-127:
The $Q_\parallel$ coordinates of these peaks correspond to the fractional indices $(0 \, 
icefrac{1 {2 )$ and $(
icefrac{1 {3 \, 
icefrac{1 {2 )$ of the reciprocal unit cell of the arachidic acid monolayer.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-128: This implies that Ce$^{3+}$ ions form a superstructure underneath the arachidic acid monolayer.
elements.sent-2411-129: The unit cell of such a superstructure has a size of $3 \times 2$ unit cells of the arachidic acid lattice.
elements.sent-2411-130: Second, the vertical size of the inorganic lattice peaks at ($Q_{z,{\rm max \sim 0.8$ \AA$^{-1}$) is significantly larger than that of the organic lattice peaks ($Q_{z,{\rm max \sim 0.4$ \AA$^{-1}$), indicating a greater vertical spatial localization of Ce$^{3+}$ ions compared to that of the arachidic acid molecules.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-131: The formation of the Ce$^{3+}$ layer at the subphase interface below the monolayer is also supported by the XSW measurements, as will be discussed in the next section.
elements.sent-2411-132: We will refer to the superstructure of Ce$^{3+}$ ions as the {\it inorganic superstructure .}

elements.sent-2411-223: The angular dependence of fluorescence intensity is known to be highly sensitive to the position of the atoms emitting secondary radiation signal, that allows to locate the atoms by the fitting the corresponding fluorescence curve.
elements.sent-2411-224: For quantitative analysis of XSW data the fluorescence intensity curves are typically simulated using the Parratt equations~\cite{parratt1954surface or optical transfer matrices (see~\cite{gibaud2000reflectivity, nikolaev2023grazing among others).}}

elements.sent-2411-365: We investigated the effect of aqueous Ce$^{3+}$ ions on the structural reorganization of arachidic acid monolayers at different temperatures and surface pressures.
elements.sent-2411-366: The morphology of the monolayer was observed by BAM.
elements.sent-2411-367: The structures were deduced from synchrotron measurements: the lateral ordering was studied with the GID and the vertical distribution of Ce ions with the XSW.
elements.sent-2411-368: By varying the surface pressure and temperature, we observed the structural reorganization of the system.
elements.sent-2411-369: In these observations we reproduced a number of known Ce-induced effects on fatty acid self-assembly.
elements.sent-2411-370: First, the binding of the metal to the carboxylic acid groups leads to the formation of a rigid monolayer with higher crystallinity and reduced molecular area, and such solid-phase monolayers are formed during compression by bypassing the liquid phase, as evidenced by the $\pi$-$A$~isotherm measurements.
elements.sent-2411-371: Second, by interacting with the carboxylic acid groups, the Ce ions may form a laterally ordered layer underneath the organic monolayer.
elements.sent-2411-372: In the GID measurements we observed an inorganic superstructure of Ce ions which is commensurate with the fatty acid monolayer having a $3 \times2 $ unit cell.
elements.sent-2411-373: The localization of this Ce layer underneath the fatty acid monolayer is confirmed by the XSW measurements.
elements.sent-2411-374: The formation of such an inorganic Ce superstructure can be reproducibly observed at $T = 23^\circ$C.
elements.sent-2411-375: The third Ce-induced effect was observed in the solid-phase monolayers formed at a lower temperature: $T = 21^\circ$C.
elements.sent-2411-376: Initially, the system behaves like a solid phase at $T = 23^\circ$C.
elements.sent-2411-377: However, with time, another superstructure appears: an organic superstructure.
elements.sent-2411-378: This superstructure does not consist of Ce ions.
elements.sent-2411-379: Instead, it is formed by the molecules of a fatty acid itself.
elements.sent-2411-380: At lower temperatures, the energy stored in the rotational motion of the molecules is reduced, allowing the mutual orientation of adjacent aliphatic chains, which in turn allows the molecular area to be further reduced, forming an even denser monolayer.
elements.sent-2411-381: When the angle between the aliphatic chains of adjacent molecules is about $40^\circ$, such a configuration is called PHB.
elements.sent-2411-382: This is an organic superstructure with $3 \times 1$ cells.
elements.sent-2411-383: Consequently, this breaks the symmetry in the structure factor and an additional peak appears in the GID pattern, which we actually observed.
elements.sent-2411-384: Furthermore, the crystal structure we deduced from the GID pattern corresponds to the PHB phase mode according to the literature.
elements.sent-2411-385: Finally, we observed a new collapse mode of AA monolayers.
elements.sent-2411-386: Surprisingly, in this collapse mode the monolayer maintains its translational order despite being compressed beyond the collapse point.
elements.sent-2411-387: Instead, the collapse is manifested by the distortion of the long-range orientational order.
elements.sent-2411-388: This is hinted at in BAM images, where a mosaic-like morphology of the film can be seen in the new collapse mode.
elements.sent-2411-390: Interpretation of the GID data leads to the following conclusions.
elements.sent-2411-391: The monolayer in the new collapse mode consists of 2D crystalline domains that are inclined from the horizontal position.
elements.sent-2411-392: The crystalline structure of a domain corresponds to the PHB packing mode.
elements.sent-2411-393: The domains are inclined according to a normal distribution.
elements.sent-2411-394: We deduced this by fitting forward simulations based on the DWBA to the GID data.
elements.sent-2411-395: Unlike the other collapse modes, the GID pattern did not decay with time.
elements.sent-2411-396: The only change in GID we observed can be attributed to a change in the distribution of domain inclination, the translational order, i.e. crystalline structure of the domains themselves, remained unchanged.
elements.sent-2411-397: By comparing AA monolayers in solid phase and in collapse, we can assume that the packing mode of a solid phase can influence which collapse scenario is realized.
elements.sent-2411-398: Namely, PHB seems to lead to collapse mode 2.
elements.sent-2411-399: Speculatively, the PHB, being a tighter packing than the free-rotating state of the AA monolayer, may explain that the translational order is unchanged in collapse.
elements.sent-2411-400: This is a subject for further study.

elements.sent-2411-524: For the simulation we consider the following parameters: average domain size, lattice parameters, tilt angle, effective thickness of the monolayer and standard deviation of the slope $\sigma_\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-525: First, we consider the GID pattern measured 5~hours after compression in Fig.~\ref{fig:series4 ~(c) [also shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:SIM4 ~(a) for reference].}}
elements.sent-2411-526: The best-fit simulation is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:SIM4 ~(b).}
elements.sent-2411-527: Note that peak rounding is adequately reproduced in the simulation.
elements.sent-2411-528: In other words, the corrugated monolayer model can quantitatively reproduce the experiment.
elements.sent-2411-529: On average, the slope of the domain is $0^\circ$ (domain is oriented horizontally on the surface).
elements.sent-2411-530: This is due to the position of the intensity maximum of the rounded peaks: exactly on the horizon.
elements.sent-2411-531: The best fit standard deviation of the domain slope distribution [for the simulation in Fig.~\ref{fig:SIM4 (b)] is $\sigma_\theta = 6.6^\circ$.}
elements.sent-2411-532: This is to be interpreted as follows: approximately 70\% of the domains are inclined with a slope less then $6.6^\circ$.
elements.sent-2411-533: The GID pattern measured 24~hours after compression is different [see the same data in Fig.~\ref{fig:series4 ~(d) and in Fig.~\ref{fig:SIM4 ~(c)].}}
elements.sent-2411-534: The peaks in this pattern are partially rounded and partially straight as for the typical GID pattern of the flat monolayer.
elements.sent-2411-535: To simulate this GID pattern, we simply assumed that there are two coexisting phase modes: a corrugated and a flat one.
elements.sent-2411-536: We assumed that the diffraction on each part gives a non-coherent contribution to the final GID pattern.
elements.sent-2411-537: To account for this numerically, we introduced the ratio parameter $\gamma$ into the model.
elements.sent-2411-538: The parameter $\gamma$ describes the ratio of the corrugated area to the total area of the monolayer.
elements.sent-2411-539: The best-fit simulation for this model, with $\sigma_\theta = 8.6^\circ$.
elements.sent-2411-540: Again, the experimental data are reproduced: one can see rounded peaks together with faint straight peaks in Fig.~\ref{fig:SIM4 ~(d).}
elements.sent-2411-541: The best-fit parameters can be interpreted as follows.
elements.sent-2411-542: At this stage, 63\% of the monolayer is flat, as in the usual case.
elements.sent-2411-543: The other 37\% of the monolayer is corrugated, but with a higher slope ($\sigma_\theta = 8.6^\circ$) than in the former case ($\sigma_\theta = 6.6^\circ$).
elements.sent-2411-544: To avoid confusion, we note that the $\sigma_\theta$ parameter represents the standard deviation of the slope, and not the slope itself.
elements.sent-2411-545: Such a mixed configuration of the monolayer (corrugated and flat) is likely related to the fact that 5~hours after compression, the barrier of the trough reached its minimum position and the surface pressure of the sample was no longer maintained.
elements.sent-2411-546: Remarkably, the monolayer compressed beyond the collapse point maintained its structural ordering even 24 hours after compression.
elements.sent-2411-547: On the contrary, part of the monolayer became flat again.
elements.sent-2411-548: It is difficult to say with the data we have whether this flattening was due to relaxation of the monolayer or some other unknown self-assembly process, and this is an interesting topic for further study.
elements.sent-2411-549: These findings are confirmed by our XSW studies.
elements.sent-2411-550: As discussed above the results obtained in XSW measurements implies that during first 5 hours after the compression beyond the collapse point (while keeping the surface pressure constant) the collapse events in AA film manifested themselves in an increase in the thickness of the Ce distribution above the liquid surface or/and an enhancement of the fractional amount of the Ce atoms located above the liquid surface.
elements.sent-2411-551: After that, when the area decreased to minimum value and the surface pressure fell down, the thickness of the Ce distribution above the liquid surface decreases or/and the fractional amount of the Ce atoms located above the liquid surface reduces.
elements.sent-2411-552: A final point to be addressed regarding collapse mode~2 is the fact that no inorganic superstructure of Ce$^{3+}$ ions was observed in the GID data.
elements.sent-2411-553: This observation is not consistent with the XSW measurements, which prove the localization of Ce$^{3+}$ ions beneath the AA monolayer.
elements.sent-2411-555: The first is that the Ce layer is amorphous in collapse mode~2, so there is no diffraction.
elements.sent-2411-556: And the second is that the diffraction is attenuated by the distortions introduced into the Ce layer by the corrugated AA monolayer.
elements.sent-2411-557: In this case, one must explain why the diffraction from the AA structure is detectable while the diffraction from Ce is not.
elements.sent-2411-558: This may be due to the difference in effective size and shape of the scattering objects: AA molecules are much larger than the thickness of the Ce layer, and the same vertical distortions may affect the diffraction from these two structures differently.
elements.sent-2411-559: Ce-induced effects on structural reorganization during self-assembly in fatty acid monolayer were investigated depending on temperature.
elements.sent-2411-560: We examined the phase behavior of AA monolayer formed on the aqueous solution of Ce(NO$_3$)$_3 \, \cdot \,$6H$_2$O salt.
elements.sent-2411-561: GID and XSW measurements were applied to follow the changes in monolayer packing in condensed and collapsed phases at two different temperatures $T=21^\circ\rm{C}$ and $T=23^\circ\rm{C}$.
elements.sent-2411-562: The Ce$^{3+}$ ions were found to form a laterally ordered layer underneath the solid-phase monolayer in both cases.
elements.sent-2411-563: GID measurements revealed the formation of an inorganic superstructure of Ce$^{3+}$ ions which is commensurate with the fatty acid monolayer having a $3\times2$ superstructure.
elements.sent-2411-564: The localization of the Ce layer underneath the fatty acid monolayer was confirmed by XSW measurements.
elements.sent-2411-565: The structural organization of the fatty acid molecules in the presence of Ce$^{3+}$ ions at $T = 23^\circ\rm{C}$ was found to be very typical.
elements.sent-2411-566: In fact, they form a lattice in the solid phase with an NN tilt.
elements.sent-2411-567: The only difference is that the cell is oblique and the packing is tighter as compared to the monolayer on pure water.
elements.sent-2411-568: Standard collapse behavior is also observed for the fatty acid monolayer at $T=23^\circ\rm{C}$.
elements.sent-2411-569: In terms of GID, this is manifested by the broadening of the peaks as the structure degrades.
elements.sent-2411-570: The most pronounced Ce-induced effects on AA self-assembly were observed at $T=21^\circ{\rm C}$.
elements.sent-2411-571: In the condensed phase ($\pi=20$~mN/m) we detected the gradual transition from the NN tilted structure to the so-called PHB packing mode -- an organic $3\times1$ superstructure.
elements.sent-2411-572: Surprisingly, in the collapse region at $T=21^\circ\rm{C}$, the monolayer maintains its translational order despite being overcompressed.
elements.sent-2411-573: Quantitative analysis of 2D maps of diffraction scattering provided the fine details of molecular rearrangement in the collapsed AA film.
elements.sent-2411-574: Namely, the monolayer in collapse mode at $T=21^\circ\rm{C}$ consists of 2D crystalline flat domains, which are inclined from the horizontal position.
elements.sent-2411-575: Importantly, the GID pattern did not decay with time.
elements.sent-2411-576: The observed changes in GID can be attributed to a change in the distribution of domain inclination, while the translational order, i.e. the crystalline structure of the domains themselves, remained unchanged.
elements.sent-2411-577: Note that collapsed structures are exceptionally challenging objects for GID studies.
elements.sent-2411-578: Nevertheless, even in such complicated cases the careful quantitative analysis of the full 2D GID pattern can provide information about fine details of mechanisms governing the collapse processes.
elements.sent-2411-579: Ce(NO$_3$)$_3 \, \cdot \,$6H$_2$O (99.99\%) was purchased from LANHIT Ltd and used as received.
elements.sent-2411-580: To form a monolayer, a solution of arachidic acid in chloroform (concentration of 0.59~mg/ml) was spread on the surface of 20~mM Ce(NO$_3$)$_3\, \cdot \,$6H$_2$O aqueous solution of pH~5.5.
elements.sent-2411-581: Chloroform was allowed to evaporate for 15~minutes, after that the layer was compressed to a desired surface pressure.

elements.sent-2411-584: BAM images were obtained using a Brewster angle microscope (model BAM-300, KSV NIMA) coupled to a temperature-controlled Langmuir trough.
